questions: Which areas do you want to discuss for Parallel Execution Engine?

answers.Which areas do you want to discuss for P: Worker pool design, CLI flag semantics, Sequential compatibility, Git worktree lifecycle

questionItems.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Parallel Execution Engine?

options.label: Worker pool design
options.description: How workers are created, assigned phases, and recycled. One ClaudeService per phase or reusable pool? (DependencyScheduler.getReady() feeds this — already built)

options.label: CLI flag semantics
options.description: How --parallel, --concurrency N, and --parallel 2,3,5 interact. Default concurrency cap? (Existing CLI has --phases flag for range selection)

options.label: Git worktree lifecycle
options.description: Branch naming, worktree location, merge-back strategy, cleanup timing. (No existing worktree code — built from scratch)

options.label: Sequential compatibility
options.description: How parallel mode coexists with sequential. Shared orchestrator code or separate paths? (Current sequential loop at orchestrator line 375)

questions: How should workers be assigned to phases?

questionItems.question: How should workers be assigned to phases?

options.label: One worker per phase (Recommended)
options.description: Spawn a new ClaudeService for each ready phase, up to concurrency limit. Simple, no reuse complexity. Worker dies when phase completes.

options.label: Reusable pool
options.description: Pre-create N ClaudeService instances that pick up phases as they finish. More complex but potentially faster startup for later phases.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the approach based on ClaudeService startup cost and implementation complexity.

questions: When the concurrency limit is reached and a phase finishes, how should the next phase be assigned?

questionItems.question: When the concurrency limit is reached and a phase finishes, how should the next phase be assigned?

options.label: Immediate dispatch (Recommended)
options.description: As soon as a worker finishes and scheduler.markComplete() returns newly eligible phases, dispatch the next one immediately.

options.label: Batch dispatch
options.description: Wait until all currently running phases finish, then dispatch the next batch of ready phases together.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what produces better throughput and simpler code.

questions: Where should per-worker metadata (worker ID, worktree path, PID, phase number) be tracked?

questionItems.question: Where should per-worker metadata (worker ID, worktree path, PID, phase number) be tracked?

options.label: In-memory only (Recommended)
options.description: WorkerPool tracks workers in memory. If autopilot crashes, resume logic re-detects state from disk (worktrees, phase completion markers).

options.label: Persisted to state.json
options.description: Write worker metadata to state.json alongside phase state. Enables precise resume after crash but adds write complexity.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on crash recovery needs and state complexity.

questions: How should questions from parallel phases be routed to the dashboard?

questionItems.question: How should questions from parallel phases be routed to the dashboard?

options.label: Phase-tagged queue (Recommended)
options.description: All questions go to a shared queue but tagged with phase number. Dashboard shows phase context. One answer file, orchestrator routes answers.

options.label: Per-worker question files
options.description: Each worker has its own questions-phase-{N}.json file. Dashboard polls all files. More isolated but more file I/O.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on existing IPC patterns and dashboard complexity.

questions: More questions about worker pool design, or move to next area?

questionItems.question: More questions about worker pool design, or move to next area?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move to CLI flag semantics

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing worker pool design

questions: How should --parallel work when no phase list is given?

questionItems.question: How should --parallel work when no phase list is given?

options.label: All eligible phases (Recommended)
options.description: --parallel with no arguments runs all phases from the roadmap, using the DependencyScheduler to determine which can run concurrently.

options.label: Require explicit phases
options.description: --parallel always requires a phase list (e.g., --parallel 2,3,5). Running --parallel alone is an error.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the most intuitive CLI behavior.

questions: What should the default concurrency cap be?

questionItems.question: What should the default concurrency cap be?

options.label: 3 workers (Recommended)
options.description: Default --concurrency 3. Each ClaudeService uses ~200-500MB. Keeps memory reasonable on most machines.

options.label: 2 workers
options.description: Conservative default. Safer for lower-spec machines but slower throughput.

options.label: Auto-detect
options.description: Base on available CPU cores or memory. More adaptive but harder to predict behavior.

questions: How should --parallel interact with the existing --phases flag?

questionItems.question: How should --parallel interact with the existing --phases flag?

options.label: Combine them (Recommended)
options.description: --phases 2-5 --parallel runs phases 2-5 in parallel (respecting dependencies). --phases alone stays sequential. --parallel alone runs all.

options.label: --parallel takes a list directly
options.description: --parallel 2,3,5 selects AND enables parallel. --phases is ignored when --parallel has arguments.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the most ergonomic combination.

questions: Should --parallel be a persistent config option (config.json) or CLI-only?

questionItems.question: Should --parallel be a persistent config option (config.json) or CLI-only?

options.label: CLI flag only (Recommended)
options.description: Parallel mode is opt-in per run. User explicitly chooses each time. Simpler, no surprise behavior.

options.label: Also in config.json
options.description: Can set parallel: true in config so --resume also runs parallel. More convenient for repeated use.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on resume semantics and user safety.

questions: More questions about CLI flag semantics, or move to next area?

questionItems.question: More questions about CLI flag semantics, or move to next area?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move to sequential compatibility

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing CLI flags

questions: How should the orchestrator handle the parallel vs sequential code paths?

questionItems.question: How should the orchestrator handle the parallel vs sequential code paths?

options.label: Unified loop (Recommended)
options.description: One orchestrator loop uses the scheduler always. Sequential mode = concurrency 1 (one phase at a time). Parallel mode = concurrency N. Same code path, different limit.

options.label: Separate paths
options.description: Keep the existing sequential for-loop unchanged. Add a parallel execution path that only activates with --parallel. More code but zero risk to existing behavior.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on code complexity and regression risk.

questions: In sequential mode (no --parallel), should phases still use git worktrees?

questionItems.question: In sequential mode (no --parallel), should phases still use git worktrees?

options.label: No worktrees in sequential (Recommended)
options.description: Sequential mode runs phases directly in the main repo, exactly as today. Worktrees are a parallel-only concept. Zero behavior change for existing users.

options.label: Always use worktrees
options.description: Even sequential phases run in worktrees for consistency. Adds overhead but simplifies the merge path (always the same).

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on backward compatibility requirements.

questions: How should --resume work when the previous run was parallel?

questionItems.question: How should --resume work when the previous run was parallel?

options.label: Resume as sequential (Recommended)
options.description: --resume without --parallel resumes remaining phases sequentially. User must explicitly add --parallel to resume in parallel mode.

options.label: Remember mode from state
options.description: State records whether the run was parallel. --resume automatically uses the same mode. Could surprise the user.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on safety and predictability.

questions: Should the unified loop use the DependencyScheduler even for the sequential --phases range case?

questionItems.question: Should the unified loop use the DependencyScheduler even for the sequential --phases range case?

options.label: Always use scheduler (Recommended)
options.description: Even --phases 2-5 without --parallel feeds through the scheduler (concurrency=1). Consistent code path, respects dependency ordering.

options.label: Scheduler only for parallel
options.description: Sequential uses simple numeric ordering. Scheduler only activates with --parallel. Less risk but two code paths.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on complexity vs consistency.

questions: More questions about sequential compatibility, or move to next area?

questionItems.question: More questions about sequential compatibility, or move to next area?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move to git worktree lifecycle

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing sequential compatibility

questions: Where should worktrees be created on disk?

questionItems.question: Where should worktrees be created on disk?

options.label: Adjacent to repo (Recommended)
options.description: Create worktrees at ../{repo}-worktrees/phase-{N}/ next to the main repo. Avoids nested .git issues and keeps repo clean.

options.label: Inside .planning/
options.description: Create at .planning/worktrees/phase-{N}/. Contained within project but adds to .planning/ bloat.

options.label: System temp directory
options.description: Use OS temp dir (e.g., /tmp/gsd-worktree-{hash}-{N}/). Clean separation but harder to find for debugging.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on git worktree best practices and cleanup ergonomics.

questions: What branch naming convention for worktree branches?

questionItems.question: What branch naming convention for worktree branches?

options.label: gsd/phase-{N} (Recommended)
options.description: e.g., gsd/phase-2, gsd/phase-3. Clear namespace, easy to identify GSD branches. Branched from current HEAD.

options.label: gsd/autopilot/{timestamp}/phase-{N}
options.description: Includes run timestamp to avoid conflicts across runs. More verbose but guarantees uniqueness.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks a clean convention that avoids conflicts.

questions: When should worktree changes be merged back to the main branch?

questionItems.question: When should worktree changes be merged back to the main branch?

options.label: Immediately on phase complete (Recommended)
options.description: As soon as a phase finishes successfully, merge its worktree branch back to main. Keeps main up-to-date, surfaces conflicts early.

options.label: All at end
options.description: Wait until all parallel phases complete, then merge all worktree branches in dependency order. Defers conflicts but risks big merge at the end.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on conflict resolution timing and Phase 3 requirements.

questions: What should happen if a merge fails (conflict) in Phase 2? (Phase 3 adds auto-resolution)

questionItems.question: What should happen if a merge fails (conflict) in Phase 2? (Phase 3 adds auto-resolution)

options.label: Fail the phase, preserve worktree (Recommended)
options.description: Mark the phase as failed, keep the worktree for manual resolution. Log the conflict. Phase 3 will add auto-resolution later.

options.label: Abort entire run
options.description: Treat merge conflict as fatal — stop all workers. Conservative but safe.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what gives the best Phase 3 upgrade path.

questions: More questions about git worktree lifecycle, or are we done?

questionItems.question: More questions about git worktree lifecycle, or are we done?

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing git worktree lifecycle

options.label: I'm ready for context
options.description: All areas covered — generate CONTEXT.md

questions: Which areas do you want to discuss for Failure Handling and Git Conflict Resolution?

answers.Which areas do you want to discuss for F: Failure modes, Graceful shutdown, Resolution context chaining, Merge conflict resolution

questionItems.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Failure Handling and Git Conflict Resolution?

options.label: Failure modes
options.description: Fail-fast vs --continue behavior. How should dependent phases react when a dependency fails? What info does the user see on failure? (ShutdownManager and requestShutdown() already exist — this extends them)

options.label: Graceful shutdown
options.description: SIGINT/SIGTERM cleanup behavior. Should failed worktrees be preserved? How are child ClaudeService processes terminated? (ShutdownManager has LIFO handlers — this wires WorkerPool into them)

options.label: Merge conflict resolution
options.description: Auto-resolution strategy for worktree merges. What conflicts can be auto-resolved vs need manual intervention? How is the resolution report structured? (mergeWorktree() currently just returns false on conflict)

options.label: Resolution context chaining
options.description: How do resolution reports from earlier merges inform later conflict resolution in the same run? What gets captured and passed forward? (GIT-05 requirement)

questions: When a phase fails in --continue mode, how should dependent phases be handled?

questionItems.question: When a phase fails in --continue mode, how should dependent phases be handled?

options.label: Skip dependents (Recommended)
options.description: Dependent phases are automatically skipped with a clear message. Independent phases keep running. Simplest and safest — no risk of running a phase on incomplete foundations.

options.label: Queue dependents
options.description: Dependent phases stay queued (not skipped). After the run, user sees which phases couldn't start and why. Allows re-running with --resume after fixing the failed phase.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the approach during implementation based on what fits the scheduler best.

questions: What should the failure output look like when a phase fails?

questionItems.question: What should the failure output look like when a phase fails?

options.label: Summary table (Recommended)
options.description: A table at run end showing all phases with status (passed/failed/skipped/running). Failed phases show error reason. Similar to test runner output — scannable at a glance.

options.label: Inline log only
options.description: Error logged immediately when failure occurs. No summary at end. User reads the log to piece together what happened.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks output format based on what existing logging patterns support.

questions: In fail-fast mode (default), should currently-running workers finish their current step before stopping, or abort immediately?

questionItems.question: In fail-fast mode (default), should currently-running workers finish their current step before stopping, or abort immediately?

options.label: Abort immediately (Recommended)
options.description: Call abortAll() on WorkerPool to kill in-progress ClaudeService processes. Fastest shutdown. Worktrees preserved for debugging.

options.label: Finish current step
options.description: Let running workers complete their current lifecycle step (e.g., finish 'plan' if mid-plan). More graceful but slower — a stuck step could block shutdown.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what ClaudeService.abortCurrent() supports cleanly.

questions: Should the --continue flag also be configurable via config.json, or CLI-only like --parallel?

questionItems.question: Should the --continue flag also be configurable via config.json, or CLI-only like --parallel?

options.label: CLI-only (Recommended)
options.description: Like --parallel, user explicitly opts in each run. Fail-fast is always the safe default. Consistent with Phase 2 pattern.

options.label: Configurable
options.description: Allow workflow.continue_on_failure in config.json. Users who always want continue behavior don't have to pass the flag every time.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on consistency with existing flag patterns.

questions: More questions about failure modes, or move to next?

questionItems.question: More questions about failure modes, or move to next?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move on to Graceful Shutdown

options.label: More questions
options.description: Dig deeper into failure mode behavior

questions: On SIGINT/SIGTERM, should all worker worktrees be cleaned up, or preserved for inspection?

questionItems.question: On SIGINT/SIGTERM, should all worker worktrees be cleaned up, or preserved for inspection?

options.label: Clean up successful, preserve failed (Recommended)
options.description: Workers that completed successfully get their worktrees cleaned. Failed or in-progress worktrees are preserved so the user can inspect or resume. Matches FAIL-04 requirement.

options.label: Preserve all
options.description: Keep all worktrees on shutdown regardless of status. User manually cleans up. Safest but leaves clutter.

options.label: Clean up all
options.description: Remove all worktrees on shutdown. Clean state but loses debugging context for failed phases.

questions: How should child ClaudeService processes be terminated during shutdown?

questionItems.question: How should child ClaudeService processes be terminated during shutdown?

options.label: SIGTERM then force kill (Recommended)
options.description: Send SIGTERM to each child process, wait a short timeout (e.g., 5s), then SIGKILL any survivors. Gives Claude Code a chance to clean up gracefully.

options.label: Immediate SIGKILL
options.description: Kill all child processes immediately. Fastest but no cleanup opportunity for Claude Code processes.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what Node.js child_process supports cross-platform (especially Windows).

questions: Should a double Ctrl+C force immediate exit (skip cleanup), or always run the full shutdown sequence?

questionItems.question: Should a double Ctrl+C force immediate exit (skip cleanup), or always run the full shutdown sequence?

options.label: Double = force exit (Recommended)
options.description: First Ctrl+C starts graceful shutdown. Second Ctrl+C within a few seconds forces immediate process.exit(1) with no cleanup. Familiar pattern from many CLI tools.

options.label: Always graceful
options.description: Every Ctrl+C runs the full shutdown sequence. If stuck, user must kill -9 from another terminal.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what ShutdownManager already supports.

questions: After graceful shutdown completes, should the process exit with code 0 or non-zero?

questionItems.question: After graceful shutdown completes, should the process exit with code 0 or non-zero?

options.label: Non-zero (1) (Recommended)
options.description: Exit 1 when shutdown was triggered by failure or signal. Makes it CI-friendly — pipelines detect the interrupted run.

options.label: Context-dependent
options.description: Exit 0 if all completed phases succeeded (some just didn't run). Exit 1 only if a phase actually failed. More nuanced but harder to reason about.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks exit code semantics based on CI best practices.

questions: More questions about graceful shutdown, or move to next?

questionItems.question: More questions about graceful shutdown, or move to next?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move on to Merge Conflict Resolution

options.label: More questions
options.description: Dig deeper into shutdown behavior

questions: What types of conflicts should be auto-resolved vs flagged for manual intervention?

questionItems.question: What types of conflicts should be auto-resolved vs flagged for manual intervention?

options.label: Disjoint files only (Recommended)
options.description: Auto-resolve when phases touched completely different files (git can merge cleanly with retry). If same file has conflicting changes, mark phase as failed and preserve worktree. Conservative and predictable.

options.label: Disjoint + non-overlapping hunks
options.description: Also auto-resolve when phases edited different parts of the same file (non-overlapping hunks). More aggressive — handles most real cases but risk of semantic conflicts.

options.label: Full auto with AI assist
options.description: Use Claude to analyze and resolve even overlapping conflicts. Most powerful but expensive (extra API calls) and harder to trust for correctness.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the resolution strategy based on what git merge supports natively.

questions: When a merge conflict is resolved (auto or manual), what should the resolution report contain?

questionItems.question: When a merge conflict is resolved (auto or manual), what should the resolution report contain?

options.label: Structured file (Recommended)
options.description: A JSON or markdown file per merge with: conflicting files, resolution strategy used, before/after snippets, and which phase's changes won. Machine-readable for context chaining (GIT-05).

options.label: Log entries only
options.description: Conflict details logged to the event stream. No separate file. Simpler but harder to use as context for future merges.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks report format based on what's most useful for downstream consumption.

questions: Where should merge resolution reports be stored?

questionItems.question: Where should merge resolution reports be stored?

options.label: Phase directory (Recommended)
options.description: Store at .planning/phases/XX-name/merge-report.md alongside other phase artifacts. Each phase owns its merge outcome. Easy to find and review.

options.label: Central merges directory
options.description: Store all reports in .planning/merges/phase-{N}-report.md. All merge history in one place. Better for cross-phase comparison.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks location based on how it integrates with existing .planning/ structure.

questions: If a merge fails and can't be auto-resolved, should the run continue (in --continue mode) with remaining phases, or stop merges entirely?

questionItems.question: If a merge fails and can't be auto-resolved, should the run continue (in --continue mode) with remaining phases, or stop merges entirely?

options.label: Continue, skip merge-blocked (Recommended)
options.description: The failed-merge phase is marked failed. Other completed phases still attempt their merges (serialized). Maximizes progress — one bad merge doesn't block unrelated phase merges.

options.label: Stop all merges
options.description: Once any merge fails, no further merges are attempted. All remaining worktrees preserved. Safest but may leave good work unmerged.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on git safety considerations.

questions: More questions about merge conflict resolution, or move to next?

questionItems.question: More questions about merge conflict resolution, or move to next?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move on to Resolution Context Chaining

options.label: More questions
options.description: Dig deeper into merge resolution behavior

questions: How should resolution reports from earlier merges be made available to later merge attempts in the same run?

questionItems.question: How should resolution reports from earlier merges be made available to later merge attempts in the same run?

options.label: In-memory accumulator (Recommended)
options.description: The orchestrator maintains a list of resolution reports in memory during the run. When a new merge is attempted, prior reports are passed as context. No disk I/O overhead. Reports are also written to disk for post-run review.

options.label: Read from disk each time
options.description: Each merge reads all existing report files from .planning/phases/*/merge-report.md before attempting resolution. Simpler (no in-memory state) but slower with filesystem overhead.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the context passing mechanism based on what's simplest.

questions: Should resolution context from a previous run (on disk) be loaded when starting a new run with --resume?

questionItems.question: Should resolution context from a previous run (on disk) be loaded when starting a new run with --resume?

options.label: Yes, load prior reports (Recommended)
options.description: On --resume, read existing merge-report.md files from disk and seed the in-memory accumulator. Gives continuity across interrupted runs.

options.label: No, fresh each run
options.description: Each run starts with empty context. Reports from previous runs exist on disk for human review but aren't fed back in. Simpler and avoids stale context.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on how --resume works with the rest of the system.

questions: What information from a resolution report is most useful as context for resolving future conflicts?

questionItems.question: What information from a resolution report is most useful as context for resolving future conflicts?

options.label: Files + strategy used (Recommended)
options.description: Which files conflicted, what resolution strategy was applied (e.g., 'took phase 3 changes for src/worker/'), and the outcome. Enough to pattern-match without being overwhelming.

options.label: Full diffs
options.description: Include complete before/after diffs of resolved conflicts. Maximum context but could be very large and noisy for later merges.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the level of detail based on what's practical for context window constraints.

questions: Should the resolution context be surfaced to the user in the summary table at run end?

questionItems.question: Should the resolution context be surfaced to the user in the summary table at run end?

options.label: Yes, brief merge column (Recommended)
options.description: Summary table includes a 'Merge' column showing: clean/resolved/conflict for each phase. Users who want details check the report file.

options.label: No, reports only
options.description: Summary table shows pass/fail status. Merge details only in the report files. Keeps the table clean.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the level of merge visibility in the summary.

questions: More questions about resolution context chaining, or move to next?

questionItems.question: More questions about resolution context chaining, or move to next?

options.label: I'm ready for context
options.description: All areas discussed — create CONTEXT.md

options.label: More questions
options.description: Dig deeper into context chaining

questions: Which areas do you want to discuss for Dashboard and Event Stream Integration?

questionItems.question: Which areas do you want to discuss for Dashboard and Event Stream Integration?

options.label: Event stream consolidation
options.description: How per-worker event files merge into a single SSE stream. EventTailer currently tails one file — needs to handle N worker files. (EVNT-03)

options.label: Per-phase status display
options.description: How the dashboard shows running/queued/done/failed per phase simultaneously. GET /api/phases currently returns flat phase list — needs parallel status states. (DASH-01)

options.label: Question routing by phase
options.description: How questions are tagged to their source phase and answered independently. FileQuestionProvider currently has no phase filtering. (DASH-02, DASH-03)

options.label: Live progress visualization
options.description: How parallel progress is shown — multi-progress-bar, timeline, dependency graph? Current progress is a single percentage. (UX of parallel visibility)

answers.Which areas do you want to discuss for D: Event stream consolidation, Per-phase status display, Live progress visualization, Question routing by phase

questions: How should the EventTailer discover worker event files?

questionItems.question: How should the EventTailer discover worker event files?

options.label: Directory polling (Recommended)
options.description: EventTailer scans the log/ directory for events-phase-*.ndjson files on each tick. Auto-discovers new workers as they start. No orchestrator coordination needed.

options.label: Orchestrator registration
options.description: Orchestrator explicitly tells the EventTailer which files to tail via an API call when a worker starts/stops. More precise but requires coupling.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the best approach based on the existing architecture.

questions: Should the consolidated stream preserve the original per-worker event files, or merge them into a single events.ndjson?

questionItems.question: Should the consolidated stream preserve the original per-worker event files, or merge them into a single events.ndjson?

options.label: Read-only fan-in (Recommended)
options.description: EventTailer reads all per-worker files and fans them into a single SSE stream. Worker files remain untouched. Simple, no write contention.

options.label: Merge into single file
options.description: A consolidator process merges per-worker files into one events.ndjson. Single file for tailing but adds write coordination.

questions: How should events from multiple workers be ordered in the SSE stream?

questionItems.question: How should events from multiple workers be ordered in the SSE stream?

options.label: Timestamp-sorted (Recommended)
options.description: Events from all workers interleaved by timestamp. Gives a true chronological view of what happened across all phases.

options.label: Per-worker groups
options.description: Events grouped by worker/phase, not interleaved. Easier to follow one phase at a time but loses global timeline.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on what the dashboard frontend needs.

questions: Should the consolidated EventTailer also work in sequential mode (single worker), or should there be two code paths?

questionItems.question: Should the consolidated EventTailer also work in sequential mode (single worker), or should there be two code paths?

options.label: Unified tailer (Recommended)
options.description: One EventTailer handles both: in sequential mode it finds one file, in parallel mode it finds N files. Same code path, zero branching.

options.label: Separate tailers
options.description: Keep existing single-file EventTailer for sequential mode, new multi-file tailer for parallel. More isolation but duplicated logic.

questions: More questions about event stream consolidation, or move to next area?

questionItems.question: More questions about event stream consolidation, or move to next area?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move on to per-phase status display.

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing event stream details.

questions: How should parallel phase statuses be represented in the API?

questionItems.question: How should parallel phase statuses be represented in the API?

options.label: Extend existing phases (Recommended)
options.description: Add parallel-specific fields to the existing PhaseState (e.g., workerStatus: running/queued/done/failed, workerId, workerPid). GET /api/phases returns the same array, richer data.

options.label: New /api/workers endpoint
options.description: Separate endpoint for parallel worker state. Keeps phase data clean but dashboard needs two calls.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks the best approach based on existing StateStore patterns.

questions: Should the dashboard show queued phases (waiting for dependencies) or only active/completed ones?

questionItems.question: Should the dashboard show queued phases (waiting for dependencies) or only active/completed ones?

options.label: Show all phases (Recommended)
options.description: All phases visible: queued (grayed out with dependency info), running (active), done (checkmark), failed (error). Full picture at a glance.

options.label: Only active + completed
options.description: Hide queued phases until they start. Simpler view but user can't see what's coming next.

questions: Should per-phase status updates be pushed via SSE events, or should the dashboard poll GET /api/phases?

questionItems.question: Should per-phase status updates be pushed via SSE events, or should the dashboard poll GET /api/phases?

options.label: SSE push (Recommended)
options.description: Emit phase-status-changed SSE events with the updated phase data. Dashboard reacts instantly. Consistent with existing SSE pattern.

options.label: Polling
options.description: Dashboard polls /api/phases every N seconds. Simpler server-side but adds latency and unnecessary requests.

options.label: Both
options.description: SSE for real-time updates, polling as fallback for reconnection. Belt and suspenders.

questions: How should failed phases be displayed — just a status badge, or expanded error details inline?

questionItems.question: How should failed phases be displayed — just a status badge, or expanded error details inline?

options.label: Badge + expandable detail
options.description: Red badge with one-line error summary. Click/expand to see full error, affected dependencies, and preserved worktree path.

options.label: Inline error always visible
options.description: Failed phases always show the full error message. No click needed but can be noisy with multiple failures.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on dashboard UI patterns.

questions: More questions about per-phase status display, or move to next area?

questionItems.question: More questions about per-phase status display, or move to next area?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move on to live progress visualization.

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing status display details.

questions: How should overall parallel progress be represented — replacing the current single percentage?

questionItems.question: How should overall parallel progress be represented — replacing the current single percentage?

options.label: Per-phase progress bars (Recommended)
options.description: Each phase gets its own progress indicator (4 steps: discuss/plan/execute/verify). Overall progress derived from sum. Clear per-phase visibility.

options.label: Single progress + phase chips
options.description: Keep one overall progress bar, but add colored phase chips/badges showing each phase's current step. Compact view.

options.label: Timeline/Gantt view
options.description: Horizontal timeline showing phases as bars: started time, current duration, dependencies as arrows. Most informative but complex to build.

questions: Should the dashboard show dependency relationships between phases visually?

questionItems.question: Should the dashboard show dependency relationships between phases visually?

options.label: Simple text annotation
options.description: Each phase shows "Depends on: Phase 1, 2" as text. No graph, just informational. Low effort, clear enough.

options.label: Arrows/lines between phases
options.description: Visual connector lines between dependent phases. Shows the DAG structure at a glance. Medium effort.

options.label: Defer to v2 (Recommended)
options.description: DASH-04 (dependency graph visualization) is already in v2 requirements. Keep Phase 4 focused on core DASH-01/02/03. Add text annotation only.

questions: Should phase step transitions (e.g., discuss → plan) be animated or instant in the dashboard?

questionItems.question: Should phase step transitions (e.g., discuss → plan) be animated or instant in the dashboard?

options.label: Instant update
options.description: Status changes immediately. No animations. Clean and functional.

options.label: Subtle transition
options.description: Brief highlight/pulse when a step completes. Draws attention to progress without being distracting.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on existing dashboard styling patterns.

questions: Should the dashboard show a summary/completion view when all parallel phases finish?

questionItems.question: Should the dashboard show a summary/completion view when all parallel phases finish?

options.label: Summary table (Recommended)
options.description: When build completes, show a summary table: phase name, status, duration, merge result. Matches the CLI summary table from Phase 3.

options.label: Just final state
options.description: No special completion view. Dashboard stays as-is showing all phases in their final state (done/failed).

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks what makes sense with the existing build-complete event.

questions: More questions about live progress visualization, or move to next area?

questionItems.question: More questions about live progress visualization, or move to next area?

options.label: Next area
options.description: Move on to question routing by phase.

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing progress visualization.

questions: How should questions from different phases be presented in the dashboard?

questionItems.question: How should questions from different phases be presented in the dashboard?

options.label: Single list with phase tags (Recommended)
options.description: All questions in one list, each tagged with its source phase (e.g., "Phase 2: Parallel Engine"). Compact, familiar. Filter by phase optional.

options.label: Grouped by phase
options.description: Questions grouped under phase headers. Each group is a collapsible section. More organized but takes more vertical space.

options.label: Tabbed by phase
options.description: Tab per active phase, each tab shows that phase's questions. Clean separation but requires clicking between phases.

questions: Should answering a question for one phase be possible while another phase's question is open?

questionItems.question: Should answering a question for one phase be possible while another phase's question is open?

options.label: Fully independent (Recommended)
options.description: Each question is self-contained. Answering Phase 2's question doesn't block or affect Phase 3's question. No modal lock.

options.label: One at a time
options.description: Only one question expanded/answerable at a time. Simpler interaction model but slower when multiple phases ask simultaneously.

questions: How should the answer file routing work — one shared answers/ directory or per-phase?

questionItems.question: How should the answer file routing work — one shared answers/ directory or per-phase?

options.label: Shared directory (Recommended)
options.description: All answer files go to .planning/autopilot/answers/{questionId}.json as today. Orchestrator routes to correct worker using the phase tag in the question. Minimal change.

options.label: Per-worker directories
options.description: Each worker gets its own answers directory in its worktree. Dashboard writes to the correct worktree's directory. More isolated but requires worktree path knowledge in dashboard.

questions: Should the dashboard show a notification badge/count for pending questions per phase?

questionItems.question: Should the dashboard show a notification badge/count for pending questions per phase?

options.label: Yes, per-phase badge (Recommended)
options.description: Each phase card shows a badge with pending question count (e.g., "2 questions"). User can see at a glance which phases need attention.

options.label: Global count only
options.description: Single badge with total pending questions across all phases. Simpler but user has to scan the list to find which phase needs input.

options.label: You decide
options.description: Claude picks based on dashboard patterns.

questions: More questions about question routing, or move on?

questionItems.question: More questions about question routing, or move on?

options.label: I'm ready for context
options.description: All areas discussed. Create CONTEXT.md with the captured decisions.

options.label: More questions
options.description: Continue discussing question routing details.
